talk_metadata.talk_title: Highplex 3D Microscopy and Spatial Profiling Applications
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "If you go to around 35 to 40 microns, about 70% of cells in a typical epithelial cancer will now be intact."
Speaker said: "We get a very high rate of misidentification of critical immune cell lineages when chopping a cell into many slices."
Speaker said: "We don't typically know what the antigen is, and it may in fact be quite diverse."
Speaker said: "There is a big value in embedding even high plex molecular in traditional histopathology images."

key_data_gaps: No quantitative comparison to standard 2D clinical assays
No validation of functional synapse identification against orthogonal methods

crispro_opportunity.description: Speaker highlighted that 5‑µm sections miss >70% of intact cells and lead to misidentification of immune lineages, indicating a need for better biomarker capture in routine pathology.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "If you go to around 35 to 40 microns, about 70% of cells in a typical epithelial cancer will now be intact. And if you chop a cell into many slices, you're going to miss the vast majority of the LAG‑3, for example."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the impact of section thickness on biomarker detection and propose optimal thickness or computational deconvolution to recover lost signals.

crispro_opportunity.description: The need to integrate 3D morphological data with functional readouts (e.g., granzyme B orientation) creates an opening for CrisPRO's multi‑modal computational spine to fuse imaging and proteomic data.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We can determine whether they have cytolytic granules in the right place... we believe that we are capturing a functional immune synapse."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can align 3D spatial coordinates with multiplexed protein quantification to generate predictive models of cytotoxic activity.

unresolved_questions: How reliably can 3D thick‑section imaging distinguish functional immune synapses versus mere proximity?
What is the optimal trade‑off between tissue thickness and antibody penetration for clinical workflows?

talk_metadata.talk_title: AI Approaches to 3D Imaging
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "AI has changed the game, the fact that a lot of these imaging methods date back as long ago as the 1850s, and why, in my opinion, it's AI and quantitative imaging that has made this the booming field that it is today."
Speaker said: "We can take a block of human tissue, section it all the way down the block and label it with AI, but we wouldn't have been able to quantify it until the 2020s."
Speaker said: "The biggest downside is that 3D imaging is tedious and expensive, so it's infeasible to think of studies on the scale of thousands of tissues."

key_data_gaps: Lack of large‑scale, publicly available 3D imaging datasets for model training
Limited quantitative benchmarks comparing AI‑derived metrics to pathologist ground truth

crispro_opportunity.description: Speaker noted that current 3D imaging studies are limited by cost and scale, suggesting an opportunity for CrisPRO to provide computationally efficient synthetic 3D reconstructions that reduce experimental burden.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "The biggest downside is that 3D imaging is tedious and expensive, so it's infeasible to think of studies on the scale of thousands of tissues."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate high‑fidelity synthetic 3D volumes from sparse 2D sections, enabling larger cohort analyses without full 3D acquisition.

crispro_opportunity.description: The presentation highlighted foundation models and synthetic staining as emerging tools; CrisPRO can integrate these with its spine to deliver end‑to‑end pipelines for multimodal tissue atlasing.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We use pathology foundation models... we can take H&E and make it into synthetic multiplex IHC using a diffusion model."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s modular architecture can incorporate foundation model embeddings and synthetic staining generators to streamline multimodal analysis.

cited_competitors.name: Meta Segment Anything Model
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as a backbone for segmentation across scales

cited_competitors.name: Open‑top light‑sheet imaging
cited_competitors.context: Cited as a method for whole‑piece imaging of needle biopsies

unresolved_questions: How can AI models be validated across diverse tissue types and imaging platforms?
What standards are needed for sharing large 3D imaging datasets to accelerate foundation model development?

companies_to_monitor: NVIDIA (GPU acceleration for AI in pathology)

talk_metadata.talk_title: Integrating Digital Pathology and AI with Molecular Pathology: Opportunities and Challenges
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we're at the very peak of inflated expectations"
Speaker said: "I predict it will go even higher"
Speaker said: "we won't replace MSI testing with an H&E predictor"
Speaker said: "the number of less than optimal samples that come to pathology is extremely large, and the expectation that foundation models ... are going to be able to handle ... is a little naive"
Speaker said: "we need rigorous performance metrics more than we're currently using"

key_data_gaps: No prospective clinical trial data presented for AI‑based mutation prediction
No head‑to‑head comparison with standard molecular assays
Limited discussion of performance on low‑quality or cytology specimens

crispro_opportunity.description: Many AI models show modest AUCs (0.6‑0.7) for mutation prediction; a high‑confidence, CrisPRO‑validated biomarker panel could stratify patients for targeted testing.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted: "the best predictor ... is MSI with an AUC of about 0.9, but for NRAS or PIK3CA it falls to 0.5‑0.6"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑modal embeddings and apply rigorous cross‑validation to improve predictive power beyond current AUROCs.

crispro_opportunity.description: Confounding factors such as biopsy type and tumor heterogeneity cause false positives; CrisPRO could model these variables to isolate true mutation signals.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "fluid samples and small biopsies drive ALK‑positive calls even when morphology is uninformative"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s probabilistic framework can weight sample quality metrics, reducing bias from low‑quality slides.

crispro_opportunity.description: Spatial transcriptomics and multiplex IF generate rich data; partnering with CrisPRO to fuse these modalities could create next‑generation predictive biomarkers.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described: "combining immunofluorescence with transcriptomics and H&E ... could predict immune checkpoint response"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s multimodal integration engine can jointly analyze H&E embeddings, IF protein panels, and spatial transcriptomics to derive composite scores.

What standards and benchmarks are needed for AI‑based diagnostic tools?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Award Lecture: Translational Epidemiology in Prostate Cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I said out loud that we'll never be able to study this in a prospective way."
Speaker said: "We tried to go through the process of patenting it, but then it turned out we gave away the international rights to patent."
Speaker said: "It's not yet translatable to standard clinical labs. So this is not a tool that's being used."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Assay requires single‑cell fluorescence imaging that is labor‑intensive and cannot be scaled for routine clinical use

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that international patent rights were lost after a conference presentation, indicating weak IP protection
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires specialized fluorescence microscopy and image‑analysis pipelines, limiting scalability
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing a prognostic test without a clear path to clinical assay validation dilutes translational impact

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that variability in cancer‑cell telomere length combined with short stromal telomeres predicts a 14‑fold increase in prostate‑cancer death, yet concluded that the assay is not yet translatable to standard clinical labs

key_data_gaps: No prospective validation of the telomere biomarker in a randomized clinical trial
No assay standardization for clinical laboratories
Lack of mechanistic experiments linking telomere variability to therapeutic resistance

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can develop a computational pipeline to quantify telomere length variability from standard histology images, addressing the scalability bottleneck of the current FISH assay.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "It's not yet translatable to standard clinical labs. So this is not a tool that's being used."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage deep‑learning image analysis to infer telomere metrics from H&E, enabling high‑throughput risk stratification.
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: Identify trials with archived prostate tissue and outcomes for retrospective validation
PubMed: Studies linking telomere metrics to prostate cancer outcomes

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO could partner to rescue the lost telomere biomarker IP by creating a novel, patent‑eligible algorithmic implementation.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We gave away the international rights to patent."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Generate a new IP layer around the computational extraction of telomere features, providing defensible assets.
external_validation_needed: Patent databases: assess freedom‑to‑operate for telomere‑related computational methods

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to model how telomere variability interacts with statin or digoxin exposure, informing combination prevention strategies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker discussed inverse associations of statins and digoxin with lethal prostate cancer.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate drug exposure data with telomere biomarker predictions to identify synergistic prevention cohorts.
external_validation_needed: ARIC cohort data with medication records and telomere measurements

cited_competitors.name: Digoxin
cited_competitors.context: Drug repurposing screen identified inverse association with prostate cancer risk

Can the telomere biomarker be measured reliably in routine pathology labs?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Prevention, Early Detection, Population Sciences, and Disparities Research Highlights
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "while aspirin has been shown to improve survival when given to Stage 1 to 3 colorectal cancer patients, it actually has the opposite effect and reduces survival when given to Stage 4 patients"
Speaker noted: "the uptake of salpingectomy in the US remains low, and there are persistent disparities"
Speaker admitted: "I’m sorry I couldn’t include all the slides that were sent to me because of time constraints"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Aspirin in Stage IV colorectal cancer
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Paradoxical survival reduction in metastatic disease without identified molecular mechanism
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "while aspirin has been shown to improve survival when given to Stage 1 to 3 colorectal cancer patients, it actually has the opposite effect and reduces survival when given to Stage 4 patients"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Opportunistic salpingectomy for ovarian cancer prevention
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Low real‑world adoption due to insurance and education barriers rather than drug/target biology

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Platelet activation reduction to prevent colorectal cancer progression
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No pre‑selection of patients based on platelet activation biomarker (UTXM) despite biomarker hypothesis
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: UTXM platelet activation score
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential under‑powering for subgroup analyses without biomarker stratification

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented scRNA‑seq data showing aspirin reduces platelet activation (mechanistic benefit) but concluded that aspirin may be harmful in Stage IV disease without explaining the mechanistic disconnect

key_data_gaps: No mechanistic explanation for aspirin paradox in metastatic disease
Lack of prospective data on salpingectomy uptake and impact on ovarian cancer incidence

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify and validate a platelet activation biomarker (e.g., UTXM) to select patients who will benefit from aspirin chemoprevention
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "she's currently evaluating a biomarker, UTXM, which is a biomarker of platelet activation, to see if this can inform who will versus who will not respond to aspirin"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate platelet‑derived omics with clinical outcomes to develop a predictive model for aspirin benefit
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: review ADD ASPIRIN trial protocol and results
PubMed: platelet activation signatures in colorectal cancer

crispro_opportunity.description: Use computational modeling to predict impact of insurance coverage and education interventions on salpingectomy adoption
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted: "insurance coverage and inadequate patient education were the primary barriers"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate population‑level uptake scenarios and estimate cancer incidence reduction
external_validation_needed: CMS policy documents on preventive surgeries
Real‑world claims data on salpingectomy rates

cited_competitors.context: Discussed as having failed to meet clinical utility endpoints

cited_competitors.name: Sotorasib and adagrasib (KRAS G12C inhibitors)
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as approved agents that newer KRAS G12C inhibitor elacestrant aims to improve upon

unresolved_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the aspirin survival paradox in Stage IV colorectal cancer?
How can insurance and education barriers to opportunistic salpingectomy be effectively overcome?

talk_metadata.session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_metadata.talk_title: In Vivo CAR T Cell Platforms and Paradigms
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: 'I'm actually not going to show that much data from my own group because this is a brand new, exciting field'
Speaker said: 'I have no idea what's going on with it [the company], and so I clearly have no disclosures'
Speaker said: 'This is mostly speculative, by calculation and with some extrapolations'
Speaker said: 'I'll take your word for it' regarding complex data slides

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Low LDL receptor expression and low dNTP pools in resting T cells prevent efficient transduction without prior activation.
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: successful reverse transcription requires a considerable dNTP pool, which is low in resting T cells... this remains actually unaddressed
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Mentioned a clinical trial in China where 5/5 patients had grade 3 AEs and one patient had an early death, potentially due to innate immune response to viral particles or CD3 cross-linking.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Immunogenicity of the CAR construct leading to anti-CAR antibodies.
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Ultimately, a lot of the animals had developed anti-CAR antibodies detected by 7 to 14 days... the B-cells came back, basically.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that standard VSVG is limited by LDL receptor expression; the 'blinded' mutation strategy is required to prevent non-specific transduction of all cells.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on CD3 cross-linking for transduction may cause significant systemic inflammation/toxicity.

key_data_gaps: No head-to-head comparison between ex vivo and in vivo potency
Lack of granularity in cytokine profiles to distinguish between infusion reactions and CRS
Biodistribution and PK not well known for in vivo approaches

crispro_opportunity.description: Overcoming the need for T cell activation (which is required for LDLR upregulation and dNTP pool increase) to allow direct in vivo transduction of resting T cells.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: successful reverse transcription requires a considerable dNTP pool, which is low in resting T cells
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Computational identification of alternative entry receptors or metabolic modifiers to bypass the dNTP/LDLR requirement for resting T cells.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: SAM HD1 inhibition and dNTP pool expansion in resting T cells

cited_competitors.name: Cilta-Cel
cited_competitors.context: Used as a benchmark for toxicity profiles.

unresolved_questions: Whether tissue-resident immune effector cells (T cells in spleen, bone marrow) are better suited for cancer immunotherapy than circulating T cells.
Whether the early toxicity in in vivo trials is due to innate immune response to particles or TCR cross-linking.

talk_metadata.session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_metadata.talk_title: CAR T Cells for Pediatric Brain Tumors
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: 'The bad news is... we saw some significant toxicity'
Speaker said: 'We didn't catch on to this until too late [regarding lymphodepletion in Arm B]'
Speaker said: 'I think we need to add immunogenicity to this list [of solid tumor failures]'

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: GD2 CAR T (Arm B - ICV without lymphodepletion)
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Host immune response (anti-CAR antibodies and T cell responses) leading to rapid clearance of CAR T cells in the CNS.
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Patients had transient responses, but they would rarely last longer than two to three months... we really felt we had lost our efficacy.
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Patient 10 achieved a complete response ongoing for over four years (Arm A).
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Tumor inflammation-associated neurotoxicity (swelling of the tumor leading to increased intracranial pressure).

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: GD2 targeting in pediatric brain tumors
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Removal of lymphodepletion in Arm B led to loss of efficacy due to immune rejection of the CAR T cells.
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Anti-CAR antibody titers (not monitored in real-time during Arm B)
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: Failure to monitor anti-CAR antibody onset temporally correlated with CAR T disappearance.
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Single-arm trial caveats.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: The use of a 1980s mouse-derived binder is a primary driver of the robust anti-CAR immune responses (CD4, CD8, and antibodies).
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on repeated lymphodepletion to prevent antibody-mediated rejection.

key_data_gaps: Lack of power to distinguish phenotypic differences between CNS-retained and peripheral-trafficking CAR T cells
Lack of data on naturally occurring GD2 binding proteins

crispro_opportunity.description: Replacing mouse-derived scFv with fully humanized or 'stealth' platforms to prevent the anti-CAR immune response that limits persistence in the CNS.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: these mapped in large part to the mouse-derived single-chain Fv... these data suggest that perhaps we should be using stealth platforms
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Computational design of non-immunogenic humanized binders and junctional domains to eliminate T cell and B cell epitopes.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: Humanized GD2 binders and their immunogenicity profiles

unresolved_questions: Whether the myeloid cells in the CNS contribute to a second axis of resistance.
Whether the anti-CAR response is initiated in the CNS or peri-CNS tissues (IgA bias suggests CNS/gut axis).

talk_metadata.session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_metadata.talk_title: Precision Targeted Engineering of CAR T Cells
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: 'this is unpublished, this data'
Speaker said: 'we're getting peripheral expression through that promoter... we propose that this loss of gene regulation may be due to disruption of the RG16 gene'
Speaker said: 'I don't have time to go into that data today [regarding 3' UTR]'

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of tissue specificity leads to peripheral expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-12), causing systemic toxicity.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: NFAT system caused rapid weight loss and systemic toxicity in mice.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Disruption of the endogenous RGS16 gene during CRISPR knock-in leads to loss of gene regulation and unintended peripheral expression.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: High expression in the spleen leading to systemic toxicity when driving IL-12.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Standard knock-in disrupts the target gene, which can lead to loss of regulatory control (e.g., RGS16). The speaker's 'next-generation' P2A linker strategy preserves the endogenous gene to maintain specificity.

key_data_gaps: No human clinical data presented
Lack of testing against other CAR targets (e.g., CD19) to check for tonic signaling effects

crispro_opportunity.description: Using tumor-specific promoters (NR4A2 for safety, RGS16 for potency) to drive armored payloads (IL-12, IL-2) specifically at the tumor site.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: NR4A2 was driving a safe delivery of IL-12 to the tumor, inducing strong anti-tumor effects
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Computational identification of more precise tumor-specific promoters and 3' UTR elements to further restrict expression and avoid peripheral toxicity.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: NR4A2 and RGS16 expression patterns in human solid tumors

unresolved_questions: Whether the RGS16 promoter is driven by TCR signaling or other microenvironmental factors.
Whether the P2A linker strategy that retains RGS16 expression might drive T cell exhaustion over time.

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center
talk_metadata.talk_title: Mechanistic Insights into CTLA-4 Blockade and Emerging Immunotherapy Biology
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I could give you some mechanistic interpretations, but the other explanations-- But the other possibility was there are other checkpoints."
Speaker said: "Adverse events with CTLA-4 are more frequent than they are with PD-1. They're essentially the same kinds, but a lot more frequent."
Speaker said: "We could not find these ICOS‑positive CD4 cells in untreated patients or mice; they only appear after anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment."
Speaker said: "We don't know exactly what the macrophages are doing in the tetrads – we think they are scaffolding, but it's not certain."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of predictive biomarkers to identify patients who will tolerate and benefit from CTLA-4 blockade
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "But the other 80%? Why didn't they respond?"
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker said: "A subset of patients experienced long‑term, durable remissions lasting more than a decade."
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker said: "Adverse events with CTLA-4 are more frequent than they are with PD-1."

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted that efficacy depends on emergence of ICOS‑positive CD4 cells, a biomarker not incorporated into current labeling, suggesting the therapeutic window is limited to patients who can generate this response.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing broad indication use without biomarker‑guided patient selection dilutes signal and increases toxicity.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that CTLA-4 blockade generates a unique ICOS‑high CD4 population, yet concluded that "CTLA-4 monotherapy can cure about 20% of melanoma patients" without acknowledging that the cure rate is confined to the ICOS‑positive subset.

key_data_gaps: No overall survival data presented for combination CTLA-4/PD-1 trials in the current talk
Lack of prospective biomarker‑stratified analysis for ICOS‑positive CD4 cells
Limited data on the role of macrophages and NK cells within tetrads

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify and validate ICOS‑positive CD4 T cells as a predictive biomarker for CTLA-4 blockade response, enabling enrichment of responsive patients.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We could not find these ICOS‑positive CD4 cells in untreated patients or mice; they only appear after anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell RNA‑seq and spatial proteomics to develop a robust ICOS‑CD4 signature and predict responders before therapy.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage ICOS ligand agonism on dendritic cells to boost CTLA-4 efficacy, as demonstrated by complete tumor eradication when ICOS‑L was added.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Adding ICOS ligand to the vaccine increased antitumor effect from essentially none to 100%."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the synergistic impact of ICOS‑L agonists with CTLA-4 blockade across tumor types to prioritize preclinical combos.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design trials that stratify patients by baseline tetrad density or ICOS‑high CD4 prevalence to avoid diluting efficacy signals.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Tetrads are more predictive of response than tertiary lymphoid structures."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's spatial analytics to quantify tetrad metrics in archival samples and define enrollment thresholds.

cited_competitors.name: VISTA and LILRB checkpoint programs
cited_competitors.context: Cited as emerging myeloid‑targeted checkpoints under investigation.

unresolved_questions: What specific ligand–receptor interactions mediate macrophage scaffolding within the tetrads?
Do NK cells participate in tetrad formation or function, and if so, how?
Can ICOS‑positive CD4 cells be reliably detected in peripheral blood as a non‑invasive biomarker?

companies_to_monitor: Companies developing ICOS agonists (e.g., AstraZeneca, Merck)

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre / University of Toronto
talk_metadata.talk_title: RNA biology, circular RNAs and their therapeutic potential
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't fully understand why some patients escaped"
Speaker said: "the sensitivity is not high" when discussing plasma circRNA detection
Speaker said: "it's an understudied area in terms of biomarker discovery"

key_data_gaps: No clinical efficacy data presented for circRNA therapeutics
No OS or PFS endpoints reported for the ongoing Phase 2 trial

crispro_opportunity.description: CircRNA abundance in plasma shows >1,000‑fold increase over linear RNA but sensitivity is low; a more robust assay could enable patient selection.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "there's actually over 1,000‑fold increase of the ratio of circular versus their linear counterpart" and later "we miss a lot" indicating low sensitivity.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model circRNA secondary structure and predict optimal primer/probe designs to improve detection limits, enabling a stratified biomarker assay.

crispro_opportunity.description: CircRMST stabilizes NKX2.1 protein; loss leads to reduced neuroendocrine transdifferentiation, suggesting a targetable resistance node.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "when we knock down circRMST, you will see the binding of both transcription factors decrease at ASL1" and "NKX2.1 half‑life drops from 17 h to 5 h".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict RNA‑protein interaction interfaces and design antisense oligos or siRNAs that disrupt the circRMST‑NKX2.1 interaction.

crispro_opportunity.description: Circular RNA‑based mRNA delivery shows prolonged expression in vivo compared with linear mRNA.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "using the circular form delivered via lipid nanoparticle, signal persisted to day 19, whereas linear mRNA disappeared by day 3".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can optimize circular RNA constructs for stability and translation efficiency, providing a competitive edge for RNA therapeutics.

unresolved_questions: Is there evidence that circular RNAs can replicate in cells?
How does the human versus mouse RMST interactome differ functionally?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis
talk_metadata.talk_title: Developing CAR T therapies for T‑cell malignancies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I don't know what to say, really."
Speaker said: "We thought that after seeing a few patients with T‑cell ALL die from no option, we thought that maybe we should try to push this forward."
Speaker said: "The FDA said they were impressed enough that they said proceed with the registration study."
Speaker said: "The FDA was completely fixated on CRS, and they wanted us to be extremely careful."
Speaker said: "We did a simple experiment where we deleted CD2 in a CAR and made the CAR to CD19 as a sort of a control."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Loss of CD7 surface expression on relapsed leukemic blasts (splicing abnormalities, biallelic deletion, down‑regulation) not pre‑emptively addressed
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "We noticed that some of the patients had lost CD7 expression. In fact, most of them that relapsed had lost CD7 expression."
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted immunodeficiency from targeting CD7 and CD2 but did not quantify infection‑related mortality

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Allogeneic CD7‑CAR T for relapsed/refractory T‑cell ALL
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all relapsed T‑cell ALL patients without pre‑screening for CD7 expression or other resistance biomarkers
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline CD7 expression / mechanisms of CD7 loss
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No on‑treatment ctDNA or serial flow cytometry to track CD7 loss

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Multiplex CRISPR editing of CD7 and TRAC in allogeneic donor T cells
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed detailed manufacturing steps (dasatinib early, alpha/beta depletion, OKT3‑mediated cell kill) revealing reliance on a complex gene‑editing workflow that could be circumvented by alternative editing platforms
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Need for alpha/beta affinity column or OKT3‑mediated depletion to achieve <5% residual TRAK+ cells, which reduces overall yield
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad enrollment without biomarker stratification and reliance on a single antigen (CD7) despite known antigen loss

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that CD2 knockout CAR T cells are functionally impaired in vitro and in vivo, yet later pursued CD2 as a primary target for T‑cell non‑Hodgkin lymphoma without fully resolving the functional deficit

key_data_gaps: No overall survival data presented
Long‑term infection and immunodeficiency outcomes not reported
No biomarker‑stratified efficacy analysis (e.g., CD7 loss kinetics)

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patients at risk of CD7 antigen loss using CRISPR‑derived resistance signatures and serial ctDNA/flow monitoring
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We noticed that some of the patients had lost CD7 expression. In fact, most of them that relapsed had lost CD7 expression."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate genomic, transcriptomic and ctDNA data to predict CD7 loss and guide patient selection or early switch to alternative CARs

crispro_opportunity.description: Target the CD2 costimulatory pathway by epitope‑masked CD2 or CD2‑cytoplasmic domain insertion to overcome CD2‑knockout functional loss
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "When we delete CD2 in T cells and in these CAR T cells, they take a huge hit. We did not see that with CD7."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model CD2‑CAR signaling networks to design optimal intracellular domains that restore function without antigen expression

crispro_opportunity.description: Co‑administer long‑acting IL‑7 (NTi‑7) to boost CAR T expansion and persistence in T‑cell malignancies
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "The addition of just a cytokine, IL‑7, provided curative potential in multiple mouse models."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict cytokine‑CAR synergy and optimal dosing windows to maximize expansion while minimizing CRS

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate early IL‑7 dosing (days 1‑7) and prospective CRS mitigation (PI3γδ + JAK inhibition) into the registration protocol
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "If you wait 21 days, there's no benefit. If you wait 14 days, there's a diminished benefit, and you have to give it between days one and day seven for them to be markedly improved."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate trial arms with varied IL‑7 timing and prophylactic kinase inhibition to forecast efficacy and safety outcomes

cited_competitors.name: AbbVie
cited_competitors.context: Sponsor of the AACR award supporting the session

cited_competitors.name: WuJin
cited_competitors.context: Company founded by former postdoc who helped develop the CD7 CAR platform

cited_competitors.name: FDA
cited_competitors.context: Regulatory body imposing restrictions on IL‑7 timing and CRS monitoring

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal timing and dosing schedule for long‑acting IL‑7 in patients receiving UCAR T therapies?
What molecular mechanisms drive CD7 down‑regulation in relapsed patients?
Can CD2‑targeted CARs be engineered to retain function without epitope masking?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre / University of Toronto
talk_metadata.talk_title: Open Chemical Probes, Epigenetic Targets and the Path to Target 2035
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we still lack chemical tools for most human proteins"
Speaker said: "most of the compounds we identified are highly selective because each pocket is different"
Speaker said: "we've identified low micromolar drug‑like compounds that were progressable"

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker emphasized that probes are freely available with no encumbrance, limiting exclusive IP advantage
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Open tools are not pursued as proprietary drug candidates, reducing direct commercial pipeline leverage

key_data_gaps: No in‑human efficacy or safety data presented
No biomarker‑stratified clinical validation

crispro_opportunity.description: Lack of biomarkers to predict which patient‑derived organoid models will respond to specific epigenetic probes
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted: "we see some models respond to a given agent, but others don't" and "context matters"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omics from organoid screens to derive predictive response signatures for epigenetic targets

crispro_opportunity.description: Epigenetic probe resistance linked to compensatory reader domain activity not captured by catalytic inhibition
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we were unable to make catalytic domain inhibitors for lysine 36 and turned to reader‑domain targeting"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model allosteric and reader‑domain networks to design degraders that overcome such resistance

crispro_opportunity.description: Open‑access probe generation platform can be leveraged to create proprietary, AI‑guided probe libraries for under‑explored WDR proteins
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described: "we are building Aircheck AI‑ready database and using DNA‑encoded library screens for 2,000 proteins"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s computational spine can prioritize ligands from the Aircheck dataset for rapid lead optimization

cited_competitors.name: Celgene
cited_competitors.context: partnered on WDR5 inhibitor development

cited_competitors.name: AbbVie
cited_competitors.context: collaborated to define a chemical probe for EED subunit of PRC

cited_competitors.name: Novartis
cited_competitors.context: independently made an inhibitor of EED interaction and took it to clinic

unresolved_questions: What biomarkers can reliably predict response to epigenetic chemical probes across diverse tumor organoid models?
Can the open‑access probe approach be scaled to generate clinically viable candidates without compromising IP protection?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Epigenomic heterogeneity in metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we have no evidence that increased accessibility of LINE1 elements also promotes ecDNA generation"
Speaker said: "we have no evidence that increased transcription of LINEs occurs"
Speaker said: "we cannot give you the answer yet"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: LINE‑1+ chromatin variant subgroup drives AR overexpression via repeat‑derived regulatory elements
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: LINE‑1+ are intrinsically resistant to androgen receptor selective inhibitors

key_data_gaps: No OS data presented
No functional validation of repeat‑derived enhancers

crispro_opportunity.description: LINE‑1+ chromatin variants create repeat‑derived enhancers that boost AR expression, suggesting a biomarker‑guided strategy to combine AR degraders with agents targeting repeat‑derived regulatory activity.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: LINE‑1+ are intrinsically resistant to androgen receptor selective inhibitors ... high density of de novo activated regulatory elements working at that locus to enhance the expression of the androgen receptor.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model repeat‑derived enhancer‑target interactions and predict compounds that disrupt AR‑driven transcriptional programs.

crispro_opportunity.description: Patients can be stratified by LINE‑1+ chromatin variant signature to predict response to AR‑targeted therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Classification based on chromatin variants over repetitive sequences identifies a LINE‑1+ subgroup with poor response to AR inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can develop a predictive classifier integrating ATAC‑seq repeat accessibility with clinical outcomes.

cited_competitors.name: Androgen receptor selective inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Standard of care drugs used in the cohort

unresolved_questions: What is the causal mechanism linking LINE‑1 accessibility to AR overexpression?
Do repeat‑derived enhancers directly drive ecDNA formation?

talk_metadata.talk_title: SMARCA4 loss drives dedifferentiated endometrial cancer via epigenetic plasticity
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we are careful about this because we don't think it's inducing dedifferentiation per se"
Speaker said: "we think that SMARCA4 enables cell state disorder"
Speaker said: "we have no embryonic signature"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: SMARCA4 loss creates uncommitted cell states that confer chemotherapy resistance
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: tumors became resistant to carboplatin after SMARCA4 loss

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that SMARCA4 loss reduced tumorigenicity in vitro yet concluded that SMARCA4 loss drives aggressive dedifferentiated endometrial cancer in vivo

key_data_gaps: Lack of in vivo validation in immunocompetent models
No identified therapeutic vulnerability

crispro_opportunity.description: Targeting the uncommitted cell state (e.g., epigenetic modulators) may sensitize SMARCA4‑deficient tumors to chemotherapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: SMARCA4 loss enables cell state disorder ... tumors became resistant to carboplatin.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict synthetic lethal interactions with SWI/SNF deficiency and prioritize epigenetic drugs.

unresolved_questions: What upstream signals trigger the epigenetic drift observed after SMARCA4 loss?
Can immune‑competent models recapitulate the phenotype?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Adenosquamous transition (AST) in KRAS‑mutant lung cancer and therapeutic strategies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we see a high enrichment of squamous signature correlates with shorter treatment duration"
Speaker said: "NF‑κB activation is transient but essential for adenocarcinoma survival"
Speaker said: "IL‑1α secreted by P63+ cells reprograms CAFs"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: AST to squamous phenotype driven by ROS‑NF‑κB‑IL‑1 axis bypasses KRAS dependence
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: once they transition to squamous, they are become resistant to the RAS oncogene inhibitor

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: KRAS G12C inhibition in LKB1‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all LKB1‑mutant adenocarcinomas without stratifying for squamous signature or ROS status
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Squamous lineage signature / ROS/NF‑κB activation markers
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No serial biopsies to capture AST emergence

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Efficacy limited by lineage plasticity (AST) that bypasses KRAS signaling
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Pursuing KRAS G12C monotherapy in LKB1‑mutant tumors without addressing plasticity

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that NF‑κB inhibition accelerates squamous transition yet concluded that transient NF‑κB activation is required to maintain adenocarcinoma lineage

key_data_gaps: Limited patient cohort (6‑8 samples)
Lack of prospective validation of KLK6A as predictive biomarker

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a KLK6A‑based assay to identify patients at risk of AST before KRAS G12C therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: high KLK6A level really correlate with poor patient survival and squamous signature enrichment
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate transcriptomic and epigenomic data to generate a predictive score for AST risk.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine KRAS G12C inhibitors with NF‑κB modulators or IL‑1 receptor antagonists to prevent AST.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: NF‑κB inhibition accelerates squamous transition; IL‑1 receptor antagonist reduces NETs and squamous tumor burden.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model pathway interactions and prioritize combination regimens that suppress the ROS‑NF‑κB‑IL‑1 axis.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design trials that stratify by LKB1 status and include serial biopsies to monitor squamous signature emergence.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Correlation of squamous signature with poor response only observed in LKB1‑mutant subgroup.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate adaptive trial designs incorporating dynamic biomarker monitoring.

cited_competitors.name: KRAS G12C inhibitors (e.g., sotorasib)
cited_competitors.context: Standard targeted therapy being evaluated

cited_competitors.name: Androgen receptor selective inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as comparison for resistance mechanisms

unresolved_questions: What is the precise trigger linking ROS accumulation to NF‑κB activation in LKB1‑deficient tumors?
Can IL‑1β blockade alone prevent NET‑mediated squamous tumor growth?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis
talk_metadata.talk_title: Lloyd J. Old Award Lecture: Dendritic Cell Subsets, Cross‑Presentation, and mRNA Vaccine Mechanisms
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We still don't know the specific mechanisms, and we don't understand everything about how the DC1 mediates help for CD8 responses."
Speaker said: "We don't know whether or not the quality of the CD8 T cells is going to be a problem, whether the ones primed by DC2s might have weaker memory responses."
Speaker said: "We don't know if one minus two thirds is one third, but this size of the response is substantial."

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that mRNA vaccines generate CD8 responses without requiring WDFY4‑dependent cross‑presentation, then concluded that "mRNA vaccines engage both DC1s and DC2s. They break all the rules" – contradictory to the earlier emphasis on the necessity of specific cross‑presentation pathways.

key_data_gaps: No in‑human or clinical data on mRNA vaccine cross‑dressing mechanism
Lack of quantitative contribution of each dendritic subset to protective immunity
No data on potential autoimmunity from cross‑dressing

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patients whose tumors rely on DC1‑dependent cross‑presentation versus those amenable to DC2‑mediated or cross‑dressed responses to tailor mRNA vaccine strategies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that "mRNA vaccines engage both DC1s and DC2s" and that "a substantial amount of this priming occurs by cross‑dressing".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell RNA‑seq and spatial proteomics to predict dominant antigen‑presentation pathways in individual tumors.

crispro_opportunity.description: Target the WDFY4‑independent cross‑dressing pathway to overcome tumors that evade conventional cross‑presentation.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated that "the tetramer response is still very proud" in WDFY4 knockout mice, indicating an alternative pathway.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Computational modeling of alternative antigen‑processing routes can reveal novel drug targets to boost cross‑dressing efficiency.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design early‑phase mRNA vaccine trials that stratify participants by baseline DC subset signatures and type‑I IFN signatures to avoid dilution of signal.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that "type I interferons are required" for cross‑dressing and that "different lipid compositions could influence this".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO to simulate trial cohorts with varying IFN signatures and predict response rates under different dosing routes.

unresolved_questions: What cell types provide the cross‑dressed peptide‑MHC complexes to dendritic cells?
Does the route or dose of mRNA vaccine quantitatively shift the balance between direct DC expression and cross‑dressing?
Will DC2‑primed CD8 T cells exhibit inferior memory or recall responses compared with DC1‑primed cells?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Award Lecture: From Chromatin Remodeling to Therapeutic Vulnerabilities
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I didn't take time to show you this, but we made germline knockouts of the mice, and they're viable."
Speaker said: "We waited 100 days, and the tumors did not come back."
Speaker said: "I have an initial binder of DCAF5, and we have a big project going on to go after that."

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: DCAF5 ubiquitin ligase adapter targeting SWI/SNF subunits
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that only a few binders exist and the target is in early discovery, suggesting limited IP protection and high risk of alternative approaches.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a synthetic‑lethal interaction that has not yet been demonstrated in patients; early‑phase preclinical only.

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data presented for DCAF5 inhibition
Lack of pharmacodynamic biomarkers to confirm target engagement in vivo
No safety data beyond mouse germline knockout phenotype

crispro_opportunity.description: DCAF5 loss restores SWI/SNF complex activity in SMARCB1‑deficient tumors, representing a synthetic‑lethal vulnerability that CrisPRO could model to predict responders.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "When we knock out DCAF5, we see the SwitchNif complex accumulating... transcription is rescued... tumors were completely wiped out and did not recur."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate CRISPR dependency data with epigenomic signatures to prioritize patients whose tumors show SMARCB1 loss and DCAF5 dependency, enabling biomarker‑driven trial design.

crispro_opportunity.description: No biomarker was presented to identify which SMARCB1‑deficient cancers are most dependent on DCAF5, an opening for CrisPRO to develop a predictive expression signature.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that DCAF5 dependency was observed in rhabdoid tumor cell lines but did not discuss patient‑level biomarkers.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use machine‑learning on DepMap and patient transcriptomics to derive a DCAF5‑dependency score for trial enrollment.

cited_competitors.name: tazemetostat
cited_competitors.context: FDA‑approved EZH2 inhibitor for SMARCB1‑deficient cancers

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal pharmacologic modality (PROTAC, small‑molecule inhibitor) to achieve durable DCAF5 degradation in patients?
Will systemic DCAF5 inhibition cause on‑target toxicities in adult tissues despite viable germline knockout mice?
Can biomarkers beyond SMARCB1 loss predict sensitivity to DCAF5 targeting across tumor types?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Harness Insight from Chromatin Priming from Early Interception of Basal Like Breast Cancer

key_data_gaps: No human patient validation of chromatin priming signatures
No clinical outcome data linking AP1/WNT10A to risk stratification

crispro_opportunity.description: Target the AP1‑driven transcriptional program that primes tumorigenesis in heterozygous cells.

talk_metadata.session_title: AACR James S. Ewing–Thelma B. Dunn Award for Outstanding Achievement in Pathology
talk_metadata.talk_title: Quantitative Pathology: From Fluorescent Imaging to Selective Biomarkers for ADC Choice
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "If the patient's going to get trastuzumab deruxtecan, they have to have at least a zero plus or originally a one plus, not a zero in order to get that."
Speaker said: "We are still in the process of checking that. I'll tell you, there are at least six trials going on now, two of which or three of which are prospective."
Speaker said: "I think we need a fourth category now that I'm calling a selective biomarker."
Speaker said: "We are negotiating with three different companies who might commercialize it. Because if it can't get commercialized, it will never get beyond my little lab."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Chromogen intensity varies three‑fold between labs and is affected by staining day‑to‑day variability, preventing reliable quantitative discrimination of HER2 low vs. zero
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Pathologist number one disqualified 44% of patients for T‑DXd, while pathologist number 18 disqualified only 15%, showing patient eligibility depends on reader rather than biology

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that AI segmentation can misclassify lymphocytes as tumor cells ("half of the cells that are lymphocytes are considered to be tumor cells") yet concluded that quantitative AI will soon replace subjective reading without acknowledging this misclassification rate

crispro_opportunity.description: High Trop‑2 expression appears to diminish response to HER2‑targeted ADCs, suggesting a combinatorial resistance mechanism that CrisPRO could model and predict

crispro_opportunity.description: Current ADC trials enroll patients based on binary IHC scores; a trial using CrisPRO quantitative thresholds could reduce enrollment heterogeneity and improve signal detection

talk_metadata.talk_title: Award Lecture – Transformative Discoveries in Pediatric Precision Oncology
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "These transcription factors are such important drivers, and they make for very challenging drug targets."
Speaker said: "These diseases have a low mutational burden, so generally, they've not been responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors."
Speaker said: "These are rare and ultra‑rare cancers, so there's a lack of market incentive for developing drugs."

Speaker said: "We don't yet know all the rules, right? Those are both nuclear proteins. They're good ligands. Could you imagine a ligand for a target that's highly expressed, for example, in Ewing or neuroblastoma that we have a ligand for that's nuclear and hooking that up to something that recruits BRD4?"

talk_metadata.talk_title: Building and Leveraging the Cancer Dependency Map
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We are not done. We still have a lot of work to do."
Speaker said: "We identified PRMT5 as a selective dependency in MTAP‑deleted cancers."
Speaker said: "We found Werner helicase to be a synthetic lethal in microsatellite‑unstable cell lines."
Speaker said: "We see that some cell lines are still sensitive to Pelo even without the 9p21 deletion, indicating an unknown mechanism."

key_data_gaps: No clinical outcome data linking identified dependencies to patient responses
Limited validation of synthetic lethal findings in in vivo models

crispro_opportunity.description: MTAP‑deleted cancers show a PRMT5 dependency; current clinical trials lack robust biomarker selection for MTAP status.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We classified the cell lines into MTAP deleted or MTAP intact, and identified PRMT5 as a selective dependency in MTAP‑deleted cancers."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate MTAP deletion status with PRMT5 inhibitor response data to develop a predictive biomarker model.

crispro_opportunity.description: Microsatellite instability (MSI) defines a Werner helicase dependency; MSI status is not routinely used to guide Werner‑targeted therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We classified the cell lines into microsatellite stable and unstable, and found Werner to be only sensitive in MSI cell lines."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can combine MSI genomic signatures with Werner dependency scores to prioritize patient subsets for clinical testing.

crispro_opportunity.description: Paralog synthetic lethal screens reveal that dual AKT1/AKT2 knockout is required in a subset of lines, suggesting combination inhibition of AKT paralogs.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "There are a subset of cell lines that you can only see dependency if you knock out both AKT1 and AKT2."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model dual‑target inhibition efficacy and predict biomarkers for AKT paralog co‑dependency.

talk_metadata.talk_title: Expanding DepMap: Paralog Screens, Resistance Modeling, and New Phenotypes
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We are not there yet. We haven't solved cancer."
Speaker said: "We are interested in whether genetic redundancies mask druggable dependencies."
Speaker said: "We have generated a library of more than 27,000 paralog pairs and screened ~300 models."
Speaker said: "We are engineering resistance alleles (ResMAP) to study convergent mechanisms across tumor types."

key_data_gaps: Paralog double‑knockout data not yet publicly released
Limited in vivo validation of resistance‑engineered isogenic models

crispro_opportunity.description: Paralog pair dependencies (e.g., AKT1/AKT2) suggest that dual inhibition strategies could be more effective than single‑target agents.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "There are a subset of cell lines that you can only see dependency if you knock out both AKT1 and AKT2."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate dual‑target inhibition and identify biomarkers predicting co‑dependency.

crispro_opportunity.description: Engineered isogenic resistant lines (ResMAP) reveal convergent ERK hyperactivation vulnerabilities that could be targeted with pan‑RAS or ERK activators.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We found that after BRAF inhibitor resistance, cells become increasingly sensitive to ERK activation, leading to cell death."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the ERK signaling network to predict optimal timing and combination of pan‑RAS inhibition with resistance‑specific vulnerabilities.

crispro_opportunity.description: High‑dimensional phenotypic readouts (e.g., perturb‑seq‑like assays) could uncover non‑viability phenotypes missed by current DepMap screens.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We are moving beyond cell viability as a readout to high dimensional profiling to identify other important phenotypes."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s computational spine can integrate multi‑omics phenotypic signatures to prioritize targets beyond simple viability hits.

cited_competitors.name: Sanger Institute
cited_competitors.context: Parallel cancer dependency map effort; data shown to be highly concordant with Broad’s CRISPR screens.

cited_competitors.context: Funding and collaboration on surface proteomics of cell lines.
