talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Yale Cancer Center / Dartmouth Cancer Center
talk_metadata.session_title: Researcher Town Hall: Navigating Policy Challenges and Securing Funding
talk_metadata.talk_title: Opening Remarks on Policy Impact and Funding Landscape
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "If the money doesn't get out to the sites, it can't be used. It's hard to hire someone if you don't have the money in the bank."
Speaker said: "The blue shows the number of applications go up, the red shows the amount of funding goes down."

Speaker said: "I think what it would be like for me 25, 30 years ago, waiting for that first K award or R01 and not getting it the first time or the second time, being worried about that, having to support family and others. It's a problem."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Multi-year awards reduced the number of grants funded by ~400 in FY 2025, but the impact on research pipelines was not mitigated.

key_data_gaps: No quantitative analysis of how funding cuts translate to loss of specific research outputs

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify labs most vulnerable to multi-year funding cuts using computational risk modeling to prioritize advocacy and bridge funding.

unresolved_questions: How will the NIH redesign funding mechanisms to avoid future large-scale grant reductions?

talk_metadata.session_title: Researcher Town Hall: Navigating Policy Challenges and Securing Funding
talk_metadata.talk_title: Translational Science Perspective on Funding and Workforce
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Between 2010, 2019, all but one of the approximately 400 FDA‑approved drugs had their point origin in academic medical research."
Speaker said: "The average time to a new faculty getting their first R01 is really delayed. This is causing lots of problems for promotion and hiring."
Speaker said: "If we can't train the best and brightest people, we are no longer going to be able to compete with China or other areas overseas."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Delays in study‑section review and lack of transparent criteria for award decisions limit early‑career investigator success.
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "We have very difficult time hiring if we can't predict."

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker highlighted that 400 FDA‑approved drugs stem from academia, then concluded that "drug development starts with academic science. It probably always will," while simultaneously noting that delayed R01 funding is causing a loss of talent, which contradicts the claim of a sustainable academic pipeline.

key_data_gaps: No specific quantitative data on how many grants were delayed or lost

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO analytics to map grant review timelines and identify bottlenecks for early‑career investigators.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "The average time to a new faculty getting their first R01 is really delayed."

talk_metadata.talk_title: ZC3H18 truncations drive ERV RNA accumulation and oncogenesis
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I think as far as I'm aware, this is the second genetic cause of ERV elevation in cancer."
Speaker said: "We showed that Z18 trunc works in a dominant negative fashion on these RNAs."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Dominant‑negative truncations sequester ERV RNAs in the cytoplasm, bypassing nuclear decay machinery

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker demonstrated that truncating mutations lose binding to the exosome helicase but retain binding to RNA‑stabilizing proteins, revealing a modular weakness.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: No therapeutic strategy currently exploits the dominant‑negative mechanism; reliance on genetic loss makes drug targeting challenging.

crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the mutant ZC3H18 structure and predict ligands that restore exosome recruitment or promote mutant degradation.

crispro_opportunity.description: ZC3H18 loss/deletion occurs in ~30% of cancers; ERV RNA up‑regulation could serve as a biomarker for patient selection for viral‑mimicry therapies.

talk_metadata.talk_title: Splicing‑regulated LAG‑3 intron retention controls T‑cell exhaustion and enables conditional therapeutic payloads
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We identified two RNA binding proteins, at least one of which is upregulated itself upon T cell exhaustion, so appears to be one of the gene expression program of exhausted T cells."
Speaker said: "The pluses and minuses are you do discover a lot of interesting new RNA isoforms, obviously with the long read RNA‑seq as an example here."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Retention introduces an in‑frame stop codon, leading to NMD and loss of functional LAG‑3 protein on acutely stimulated T cells

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed the minigene construct and intron‑based switch, indicating the core IP is the synthetic intron design rather than a novel protein.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on chronic stimulation to activate the switch may limit efficacy in tumors with low antigen load.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that intron retention in LAG‑3 leads to loss of surface protein (observation) but concluded that LAG‑3 is a therapeutic target for checkpoint blockade (contradiction).

key_data_gaps: In vivo efficacy of the splicing‑regulated FOXO1/IL‑12 constructs beyond tumor growth curves
Safety assessment of chronic cytokine expression

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to identify small molecules that enhance the LAG‑3 intron removal, synergizing with the splicing‑regulated payloads.

crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the splicing factor‑RNA interaction and predict modulators to boost intron excision.

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate that long‑read single‑cell RNA‑seq uniquely discovers functional intron retention events missed by short‑read data.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Even in our own short‑read deep RNA sequencing data, we don't see this differential splicing event. It's really only been seen now through the use of long‑read sequencing."

talk_metadata.talk_title: Defining senescence and implications for cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "When we did senolysis or removal of senescent cells, either through molecules or through transgenic animals, we could restore the cartilage. And we observed reduction of SASP factors, reduction of pain, and we were very surprised, we also saw an increase in tissue repair when those senescent cells were removed."

Speaker said: "One of the questions that's not resolved is if you eliminate senescent cells, how much of that is that you eliminate SASP components produced by the senescent cells in the joint versus components produced by the senescent pericytes in the tumor that are driving this normalization of vasculature..."

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data presented
No biomarker‑stratified analysis of senescent cell burden in patients
No direct comparison to standard of care in cancer models

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for robust, multi‑gene senescence signatures to identify patients whose tumors or joints have high senescent cell burden before applying senolytic or senomorphic strategies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We used a genetic model ... we could see a small number of senescent cells are present... we used bulk sequencing to get a signature which we call senescence signature..."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell and spatial transcriptomics to derive composite senotype signatures and predict which patients would benefit from senolytic or senomorphic interventions.

crispro_opportunity.description: The observation that genetic deletion of p16‑positive senescent cells mitigates checkpoint blockade efficacy suggests that existing senolytic programs could be repurposed to enhance immunotherapy response.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Drew said: "If one uses a genetic senescence deletion model, the anti‑tumor effects of checkpoint blockade are mitigated."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the impact of senescent cell removal on immune checkpoint pathways and prioritize combination regimens.

crispro_opportunity.description: Senescence‑driven vascular normalization improves T‑cell infiltration; combining senolytics with anti‑PD‑1 may boost responses in osteoarthritis‑associated cancer patients.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Drew said: "If you eliminate senescent cells, you lose the phenotype of the vessel structure, and we lose the phenotype of the T cell infiltration and P‑selectin, and our tumors are no longer responding to immunotherapy."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate senolytic‑checkpoint blockade synergy across tumor types and suggest optimal dosing windows.

unresolved_questions: What is the relative contribution of joint‑derived SASP versus tumor‑derived SASP to the enhanced immunotherapy response?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Foundational models for prognostic prediction (SURFER)
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I don't claim that SURFER is the best solution. This is just the idea."
Speaker said: "Existing foundational models are very good at separating disease types but they don't stratify risk within a disease"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: existing unimodal/multimodal foundation models (e.g., Titan, CONJ)
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: absence of prognostic outcome information during self‑supervised pre‑training limits survival prediction performance

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Relies on a single vision‑language backbone; any limitation of Titan (e.g., scanner bias) propagates to SURFER
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Model trained on ~15,000 slides; may still lack rare cancer subtypes and treatment‑specific contexts

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker highlighted that foundation models enable high accuracy with little data, yet later reported that "there is not that much improvement in survival prediction over the ResNet‑50 baseline"

key_data_gaps: No external prospective validation of SURFER on independent clinical cohorts
Limited exploration of multimodal text beyond survival outcomes

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can embed survival time as a continuous label during contrastive training, directly addressing the blindspot of missing prognostic signal in current models
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We introduced SoftCLIP which weights embeddings based on survival distance"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s SoftCLIP‑style loss can be integrated to align embeddings with time‑to‑event data, improving risk stratification
external_validation_needed: PubMed: studies on contrastive learning with censored survival data
ClinicalTrials.gov: trials using AI for prognostic biomarker discovery

crispro_opportunity.description: Public benchmark PATOS can be expanded with CrisPRO embeddings to standardize survival prediction evaluation across institutions
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We need a public benchmark so new models can be compared"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can provide a unified embedding space for all participants, reducing variability in benchmark results
external_validation_needed: Citeline: track adoption of PATOS benchmark
GitHub: repository for CrisPRO embedding pipelines

cited_competitors.name: Titan
cited_competitors.context: used as backbone for SURFER

cited_competitors.name: CONJ
cited_competitors.context: benchmark best performer for survival prediction

cited_competitors.name: Virchow
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as a larger model that did not outperform smaller ones

cited_competitors.name: MuSi
cited_competitors.context: cited as a multimodal model that predicts KRAS mutations

unresolved_questions: How can multimodal text be enriched with treatment response information without over‑fitting?
What is the optimal batch size and loss function for slide‑level versus patch‑level models in survival tasks?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Technical University of Dresden / University Hospital Dresden
talk_metadata.talk_title: Clinical and research applications of pathology foundation models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We have reached human expert performance for tumor detection, the problem is solved"
Speaker said: "Yet almost nobody's using it, because digital pathology adoption is <5% in Europe"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: low adoption of whole‑slide scanners limits real‑world deployment of AI models
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted that "most pathology slides in the US are not digitized"

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Reliance on proprietary foundation models (e.g., CONCH, UniPathology) without transparent training data creates regulatory and reproducibility risk
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Strategy focuses on high‑performing AI without addressing the prerequisite digital pathology rollout

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker claimed AI detection is solved and ready for clinical use, then emphasized that <5% of pathology is digital, meaning the technology cannot be applied—a contradiction between technical readiness and practical feasibility

key_data_gaps: No prospective randomized trial data for foundation‑model‑driven prognostic tools presented
Limited evidence on regulatory pathways for autonomous agents

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can supply a standardized, bias‑aware embedding layer for multi‑center trials, reducing the need for each site to digitize at the same scanner type
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We need more infrastructure investment to enable AI in routine pathology"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s platform can generate scanner‑agnostic embeddings, allowing pooled analysis across heterogeneous sites
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: identify trials requiring AI‑based pathology endpoints
Regulatory guidance documents on AI medical devices

crispro_opportunity.description: Partner with digital scanner manufacturers to embed CrisPRO embeddings directly into acquisition pipelines
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted the bottleneck of low digital pathology adoption
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Embedding CrisPRO at the acquisition stage can provide immediate decision support without separate post‑processing
external_validation_needed: Company press releases from scanner vendors (e.g., Leica, Philips) about AI integration

cited_competitors.name: Campanella et al.
cited_competitors.context: prospective trial using AI to screen EGFR mutations from slides

cited_competitors.name: ChatGPT / Claude
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as large language models enabling agents

cited_competitors.name: UNIPATHology
cited_competitors.context: cited as a foundation model that works beyond its training domain

unresolved_questions: What specific regulatory framework will govern autonomous AI agents in oncology?
How can hospitals quantify the ROI of investing $500 M in digital pathology infrastructure?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Omega-6 fatty acids drive FABP5‑mediated mTOR activation in triple‑negative breast cancer

key_data_gaps: No clinical trial data linking omega‑6 diet to patient outcomes
Lack of mechanistic data on FABP5 source in obese patients

crispro_opportunity.description: FABP5 serum levels could be used to stratify triple‑negative breast cancer patients who may benefit from mTOR‑targeted therapies or dietary interventions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "triple‑negative breast cancer patients have elevated FABP5 in their serum" and "omega‑6 diet enhances tumor growth via FABP5‑mediated mTOR activation".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate FABP5 expression, dietary fatty‑acid profiles, and mTOR pathway activity to identify patients likely to respond to mTOR inhibitors or anti‑FABP5 strategies.

crispro_opportunity.description: Targeting the FABP5‑omega‑6 interaction with small‑molecule inhibitors could be combined with existing mTOR inhibitors for synergistic effect.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned: "We've made an inhibitor that blocks this interaction, and it works in cells and in mice".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can prioritize compounds that disrupt the FABP5‑omega‑6 binding pocket and predict combinatorial efficacy with mTOR inhibitors.

unresolved_questions: What is the precise source of circulating FABP5 in obese, tumor‑free individuals?
Does each individual omega‑6 fatty acid (linoleic vs arachidonic) differ in potency for FABP5 activation?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Structural and regulatory insights into BRAF and CRAF activation

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that mutating the dimer interface abolishes signaling, indicating that drugs targeting this interface could overcome resistance to current RAF inhibitors.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Current RAF inhibitors do not adequately address dimer‑dependent activation, leading to paradoxical activation and resistance.

key_data_gaps: No in‑vivo validation of the newly solved CRAF structures
Lack of data on how identified regulatory differences affect response to existing RAF inhibitors

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop molecules that lock BRAF/CRAF dimers in an inactive conformation to synergize with existing RAF/MEK inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We have an assay to detect inhibitors that suppress the monomer to dimer transition".
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the dimer interface dynamics and predict compounds that stabilize the inactive monomer, guiding combination design with current RAF inhibitors.

crispro_opportunity.description: Stratify patients based on BRAF N‑terminal acidic segment mutations to predict response to dimer‑targeting agents.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that BRAF’s acidic N‑terminal segment drives selective K‑Ras binding and dimerization.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate mutation‑specific structural data to define biomarker panels for enrolling patients in dimer‑targeted trials.

unresolved_questions: How do the distinct regulatory mechanisms of CRAF versus BRAF influence resistance to current RAF inhibitors?
Can the identified 14‑3‑3 binding differences be exploited therapeutically?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Autocrine LIF signaling drives immune evasion in ALKB1‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: LIF‑driven recruitment of immunosuppressive neutrophils and Arg1⁺ macrophages suppresses checkpoint blockade efficacy
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker noted that ALKB1‑mutant tumors respond poorly to anti‑PD‑1/PD‑L1 therapy without a clear mechanistic explanation.
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker described a subset of tumors with high LIF expression that are especially resistant to immunotherapy.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker referenced an AstraZeneca anti‑LIF antibody in clinical trials, indicating a competitive landscape for LIF targeting.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Monotherapy with anti‑LIF may be insufficient without combination with checkpoint inhibitors.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that LIF activates STAT3 in tumor cells (molecular observation) but concluded that targeting LIF alone can restore anti‑tumor immunity without mentioning the need for concurrent checkpoint blockade (clinical conclusion).

key_data_gaps: Long‑term survival benefit of anti‑LIF therapy not shown
Effect of anti‑LIF combined with PD‑1 blockade not evaluated

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine anti‑LIF antibodies with PD‑1/PD‑L1 checkpoint inhibitors to overcome primary resistance in ALKB1‑mutant lung cancer.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "ALKB1‑mutant tumors respond poorly to checkpoint blockade" and later showed anti‑LIF reduces immunosuppressive myeloid cells.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the synergistic impact of dual LIF and PD‑1 inhibition on the tumor microenvironment and predict patient subsets most likely to benefit.

crispro_opportunity.description: Use tumor LIF expression or phospho‑STAT3 levels as biomarkers to select patients for anti‑LIF therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted elevated LIF and phospho‑STAT3 in ALKB1‑mutant tumors.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate transcriptomic LIF signatures with phospho‑STAT3 IHC data to generate a predictive biomarker panel.

crispro_opportunity.description: Repurpose existing LIF‑targeting agents (e.g., AstraZeneca antibody) for ALKB1‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned an AstraZeneca anti‑LIF antibody in clinical trials for NSCLC.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can assess the fit of the AstraZeneca antibody’s pharmacodynamic profile with the LIF‑driven immune phenotype in ALKB1‑mutant tumors.

cited_competitors.name: AstraZeneca anti‑LIF antibody
cited_competitors.context: clinical trial in non‑small cell lung cancer

unresolved_questions: Does inhibition of LIF synergize with PD‑1 blockade in vivo?
What upstream signals drive LIF upregulation after ALKB1 loss?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Molecular and cellular alterations associated with malignant progression in the pancreas
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We have shown using more comprehensive sequencing that these really are independent clones within the same lesion."
Speaker said: "We haven't shown the relevance of this to PANMs, but I think we'll hear our next speakers talk more about the stroma around the PANMs."
Speaker said: "If surgery is our intervention, we can't be targeting low‑grade PANAM because none of us would have a pancreas anymore."

key_data_gaps: No functional validation of fibroblast subpopulations in vivo
No longitudinal outcome data linking PANM burden to cancer development
Lack of imaging biomarkers to measure PANM burden in patients

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for imaging or molecular biomarkers that predict which low‑grade PANMs will progress to PDAC.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We need really biomarkers that predict risk of progression to PDAC, not biomarkers that predict the presence of a PANAM."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO could integrate spatial transcriptomics and imaging mass cytometry data to derive a predictive risk score for individual PANMs.

crispro_opportunity.description: KRAS‑targeted vaccines or degraders could be combined with stromal‑modulating agents to intercept multifocal KRAS‑mutant lesions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Vaccines that are designed to target those same KRAS mutations could be used in the pre‑cancer setting."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic effects of KRAS immunotherapy plus stromal normalization on lesion clearance.

cited_competitors.name: KRAS inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as potential interception tools for KRAS‑mutant precancers.

cited_competitors.name: KRAS vaccines
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as low‑toxicity systemic approach for high‑risk individuals.

unresolved_questions: What molecular drivers, beyond KRAS, cause the transition from low‑grade to high‑grade PANMs?
How does the heterogeneous immune microenvironment within a single PANM influence progression?
Can non‑invasive imaging reliably quantify PANM burden in vivo?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Evolution of pancreatic cancer microenvironment
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We have about 160 donors now, with a wide range of ages, and more than half of donors even in fairly young ages have PanIN lesions."
Speaker said: "If we all have lesions but most of us don't get pancreatic cancer, why is that?"
Speaker said: "The microenvironment is completely distinct from that of cancer, which raises the question of is that restraining progression?"

key_data_gaps: Limited functional assays linking identified fibroblast subsets to tumor promotion
No prospective validation of IL6/JAK‑STAT3 activation as a therapeutic target
Absence of longitudinal donor follow‑up to correlate microenvironmental states with cancer incidence

crispro_opportunity.description: Targeting IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling in tumor‑specific fibroblasts could suppress progression of PanIN to invasive cancer.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "One of the differences was IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling, which is highly enriched in poorly differentiated tumors."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict the impact of JAK inhibition on fibroblast‑tumor cross‑talk using spatial transcriptomics data.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop spatially resolved biomarkers (e.g., phospho‑STAT3, LRC15) to identify high‑risk PanIN lesions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We validated phospho‑STAT3 enrichment in tumor‑adjacent fibroblasts by immunostaining."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate imaging mass cytometry and Visium data to generate a composite risk map for individual lesions.

cited_competitors.name: JAK inhibitors
cited_competitors.context: Implied as potential agents to block STAT3‑enriched fibroblast signaling.

unresolved_questions: What drives the switch from plasma‑cell‑rich neighborhoods in PanIN to macrophage‑rich neighborhoods in cancer?
Are the tumor‑specific fibroblast populations (fibro‑2, fibro‑3) functionally interchangeable or do they have distinct roles?
How early does IL6/JAK‑STAT3 activation appear during PanIN progression?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Stromal targeting strategies for pancreatic cancer interception
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We need to think about it both in the single cell but also in the context of everything together, the tumor mass and the human individual that is undergoing these processes."
Speaker said: "The higher host factor, the more protumorigenic, the lower the host factor, the less protumorogenic."
Speaker said: "We demonstrated that we need the host factor, and we decided to take this to the clinic."

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Normalization of tumor‑promoting stroma via altered radiation schedule
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Patients were not stratified by baseline stromal host factor, risking heterogeneous response
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline host factor score to select patients likely to benefit
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering due to lack of biomarker‑driven stratification

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that only a few antibodies were available and performance was modest, suggesting limited IP breadth.
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Manual imaging analysis limits throughput for screening.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single stromal target (netrin G1) may be insufficient given fibroblast heterogeneity.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed spatial data indicating distinct tumor‑specific fibroblast populations, then concluded that a single host‑factor metric can capture stromal protumorigenicity — ignoring the underlying fibroblast heterogeneity.

key_data_gaps: No prospective validation that host factor predicts clinical outcomes
Limited in‑vivo efficacy data for netrin G1 antibodies
Unclear how peptide antagonists affect stromal biology in patients

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate baseline host‑factor scoring as a stratification biomarker in stromal‑normalization trials.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Patients treated with continuous radiation have protumorogenic stroma; the goal for the PLDR trial is to get rid of the cancer without aggravating the stroma."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can generate a composite host‑factor algorithm and simulate enrichment of responders vs non‑responders.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage CrisPRO’s spatial‑omics integration to identify additional stromal targets beyond netrin G1.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted multiple fibroblast subsets (fibro‑2, fibro‑3) with distinct markers.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can prioritize targets by network centrality across fibroblast subpopulations.

cited_competitors.name: Netrin G1 antibodies
cited_competitors.context: Discussed as a stromal target under development.

cited_competitors.name: Radiation therapy (continuous vs PLDR)
cited_competitors.context: Compared as standard of care versus experimental stromal‑preserving approach.

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal host‑factor threshold to define stromal protumorigenicity?
Can peptide antagonists be redesigned to avoid immunogenicity while retaining activity?
How do fibroblast‑derived Wnt ligands influence epithelial KRAS‑driven lesions?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center
talk_metadata.talk_title: Archival DNA and RNA sequencing (ARCH-DR) applied to esophageal adenocarcinoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we can look at RNA species, copy number alterations, and mutations all at the same time in the same cells"
Speaker said: "we see that ecDNA expression saturates around 20 copies"
Speaker said: "about 51% of the genes are dosage sensitive, while many are not"
Speaker said: "only ~20% of somatic mutations have an impact on gene expression"

key_data_gaps: No longitudinal clinical outcome correlation
No functional validation of dosage‑insensitive genes

crispro_opportunity.description: Lack of patient‑level biomarkers linking ecDNA copy number to therapeutic response in EAC
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we see that ecDNA expression saturates around 20 copies"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model the non‑linear relationship between ecDNA dosage and downstream expression to predict response thresholds

crispro_opportunity.description: ~20% of somatic mutations drive expression changes, suggesting mutation‑specific resistance pathways
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "only ~20% of somatic mutations have an impact on gene expression"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate single‑cell mutation and expression data to pinpoint mutation‑driven transcriptional programs for targeted intervention

cited_competitors.name: 10x Genomics
cited_competitors.context: provides commercial single‑cell DNA, RNA and ATAC platforms referenced throughout the talk

cited_competitors.name: Scale Bio

cited_competitors.name: TenX Genomics
cited_competitors.context: cited in discussion of sequencing cost reductions

unresolved_questions: Does ecDNA load correlate with TP53 mutation status or expression levels?
What mechanisms underlie the ~49% of genes that are dosage‑insensitive?

talk_metadata.talk_title: High‑content single‑cell multi‑omics to resolve molecular determinants of cancer cell phenotypes
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "with PTA we are detecting about 90% of variants, which is a significant improvement over other methods"
Speaker said: "the allele frequency distribution is centered around 50% with PTA, unlike other methods"
Speaker said: "single cells from leukemia have 2,000‑4,000 mutations, far more than bulk detects"
Speaker said: "we can map drug‑resistant clones by integrating genome, surface markers and phylogeny"

key_data_gaps: Limited number of surface markers (only six) for phenotype mapping
No in‑vivo validation of resistance mechanisms

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for computational framework to integrate high‑content DNA, RNA and surface‑marker data at scale
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "the powerful part is the quantitative framework for being able to do this on larger data sets"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can provide scalable multi‑modal integration pipelines and predictive modeling of genotype‑phenotype links

crispro_opportunity.description: Current single‑cell resistance studies lack prospective trial designs that stratify patients by emergent resistant clones
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we can map pre‑ and post‑treatment clones and ask which clones were enriched"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate trial arms with clone‑specific biomarkers to optimize enrollment criteria

cited_competitors.name: 10x Genomics
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as a platform for high‑throughput single‑cell assays

cited_competitors.name: Mission Bio
cited_competitors.context: referenced as a droplet‑based approach that is not compatible with multi‑step chemistry

unresolved_questions: How do the identified surface‑marker phenotypes translate to functional drug resistance in patients?
Can the PTA method be adapted for routine clinical diagnostics given its current cost and workflow?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Single‑cell DNA methylation and multi‑omic technologies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "DNA methylation is almost always altered in cancer"
Speaker said: "most techniques are low‑throughput, so we developed SKY‑Met for high‑throughput combinatorial indexing"
Speaker said: "our latest version produced 140,000 single‑cell methylome data set in human cerebral cortex"
Speaker said: "we can combine nucleosome disruption with 10x ATAC to get whole‑genome sequencing"

key_data_gaps: Limited validation of methylation‑only cell‑type markers compared to RNA‑based markers
No FFPE compatibility demonstrated

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for methylation‑based biomarkers to complement RNA‑based subtyping in cancers where RNA signatures overlap
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "cell types are close together in methylation space, making it harder to distinguish than RNA"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate methylation and expression data to derive composite signatures that improve cell‑type resolution

crispro_opportunity.description: SKY‑Met offers combinatorial indexing without custom reagents, lowering barrier to entry versus other methylation platforms
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "no one was doing this technology when we published it because you can't get the reagents; we made it compatible with off‑the‑shelf 10x kits"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can leverage SKY‑Met data to build predictive models of epigenetic drug response, positioning the platform as a go‑to for epigenomic biomarker discovery

cited_competitors.name: 10x Genomics
cited_competitors.context: their ATAC kit is repurposed for nucleosome disruption and whole‑genome sequencing

cited_competitors.name: Salk Institute (SNM‑C‑seq)
cited_competitors.context: cited as a high‑coverage, low‑throughput methylation method requiring massive plate usage

unresolved_questions: How does non‑CpG methylation contribute to cancer cell state heterogeneity beyond neuronal contexts?
Can the methylation‑ATAC co‑assay be optimized to retain high‑quality RNA for a full triple‑omics readout?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Sydney Children's Hospital and Cancer Institute
talk_metadata.talk_title: Precision medicine in childhood CNS tumors and beyond
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we still really don't know what the biomarker is that we should be targeting"

Speaker said: "the majority who you can match to a targeted drug were actually getting some clinical benefit of some sort. But these are very high‑risk patients. They've failed multiple therapies. Are we really helping them in the long term?"
Speaker said: "we are starting to make a difference with these patients if we can find the right target and the right drug to match them"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Many targeted agents used without validated biomarkers
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of identified predictive biomarkers for most genomic alterations beyond fusions
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we still really don't know what the biomarker is that we should be targeting

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker highlighted the "magic bullet" concept of a single genomic change matched to a drug, then admitted that "the reality is most tumors have multiple pathways" – the scientific observation of pathway complexity contradicts the simplistic clinical conclusion of a single‑target approach.

key_data_gaps: No randomized controlled trial comparing precision‑guided vs unguided therapy
Limited long‑term overall survival data for many sub‑cohorts
No biomarker‑stratified analysis for non‑fusion targets

crispro_opportunity.description: Many patients receive matched drugs without a validated predictive biomarker; CrisPRO could develop computational models to prioritize actionable alterations.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we still really don't know what the biomarker is that we should be targeting"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's machine‑learning pipeline to integrate whole‑genome, RNA‑seq and methylation data to predict functional dependency on specific pathways.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combination of targeted agents to address multiple concurrent pathways in high‑risk CNS tumors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we need combination therapies. We need to be able to hit multiple pathways at the same time"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can rank synergistic drug pairs based on pathway co‑dependency networks derived from patient genomics.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design prospective, biomarker‑driven trials rather than retrospective cohort comparisons.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted the need for "clinical trials that will help us understand, can we use that prognostic information and different molecular stratification to change the outcome"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage CrisPRO's predictive response models to define enrichment criteria and adaptive randomization schemes.

cited_competitors.name: Larotrectinib
cited_competitors.context: Used as a TRK inhibitor for a fusion‑positive infant patient

cited_competitors.name: Toverafenib
cited_competitors.context: Second‑generation pan‑RAF inhibitor discussed for BRAF‑fusion low‑grade gliomas

unresolved_questions: How can we systematically identify the most predictive biomarkers for the many non‑fusion targets?
What are the optimal drug combinations to overcome the multiple‑pathway activation in high‑risk CNS tumors?

companies_to_monitor: Loxo Oncology (now part of Eli Lilly)

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Boston Children’s Hospital
talk_metadata.talk_title: Biologic subtypes and outcomes in osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "there is a clear differential in clinical benefit based on what you're actually targeting"
Speaker said: "we have been unable to improve outcomes in osteosarcoma for 40 years"
Speaker said: "pan‑RAF inhibitors are showing unprecedented clinical responses"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential extra‑chromosomal MYC amplification not addressed by current inhibitors

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted dramatic responses but noted these agents are still early‑stage and lack mature safety/efficacy data, suggesting a window for competitors.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on single‑gene alterations (MYC) without addressing underlying extra‑chromosomal DNA may dilute therapeutic signal.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker reported "no improvement in outcomes in 40 years" for osteosarcoma yet later described "unprecedented clinical responses" with pan‑RAF inhibitors, contradicting the earlier statement of stagnation.

key_data_gaps: Lack of prospective validation of MYC amplification/protein as a therapeutic target
No functional studies confirming MYC as a druggable vulnerability
Absence of randomized trials testing pan‑RAF inhibitors in osteosarcoma

crispro_opportunity.description: MYC amplification and protein expression define a high‑risk osteosarcoma subgroup that is not yet used for trial enrollment.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "MYC amplification and protein expression are associated with worse overall survival"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate copy‑number and IHC data to generate a composite MYC activity score for patient stratification.

crispro_opportunity.description: Extra‑chromosomal MYC amplification may drive resistance to conventional therapies; targeting MYC transcriptional programs could overcome this.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker speculated that MYC amplifications are likely extra‑chromosomal DNA and hard to target.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's network‑based modeling to identify downstream synthetic‑lethal partners of MYC for drug targeting.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine pan‑RAF inhibitors with MEK inhibitors to prevent paradoxical activation in BRAF‑fusion driven tumors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker discussed pan‑RAF inhibitors as a solution to paradoxical activation seen with first‑generation BRAF inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Predict synergistic drug pairs using CrisPRO's pathway interaction maps.

crispro_opportunity.description: Prospective, biomarker‑enriched trials for MYC‑amplified osteosarcoma and STAG2‑mutated Ewing sarcoma.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized the need for "clinical trials that will help us understand, can we use that prognostic information and different molecular stratification to change the outcome"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Apply CrisPRO's adaptive trial simulation to optimize enrichment criteria and statistical power.

cited_competitors.name: Toverafenib
cited_competitors.context: Lead pan‑RAF inhibitor discussed for BRAF‑fusion low‑grade gliomas

cited_competitors.name: Larotrectinib
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as a successful TRK inhibitor in a CNS tumor case

unresolved_questions: What is the exact contribution of extra‑chromosomal MYC DNA to poor prognosis and drug resistance?
Can MYC‑driven osteosarcoma be effectively targeted with indirect MYC inhibitors or synthetic‑lethal partners?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Spatial transcriptomics technologies and tumor history
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "These measurements are snapshots. You have to kill the sample, or maybe the sample's already dead, when you make the measurement, and they're destructive snapshots."
Speaker said: "We think of it as a kind of a universal platform to move any single-cell measurement, whether that is DNA, RNA, epigenomes, directly into the spatial context."
Speaker said: "We see that the neural crest transitory state is strongly associated with a particular immune suppressive neighborhood..."

key_data_gaps: No functional validation of inferred lineage relationships

talk_metadata.talk_title: Decoding tissue architecture in tumors with SEGER and Wormhole
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't want to solve this by using a boundary, we're not going to draw a membrane staining, that won't work."
Speaker said: "Our AI is going to understand the phenotypic manifold, cell states, how they relate to each other, not cell types, but the full rich manifold."
Speaker said: "Basal cells are only 4% of the tissue mass, but killing them collapses the entire tumor."

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Relies on nucleus‑centric graph embedding; may be circumvented by alternative segmentation pipelines that improve boundary detection.

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Transformer model trained on specific mouse datasets; may not generalize without extensive retraining to human clinical samples.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Requires massive compute resources; could delay translation to routine pathology.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that basal cells constitute only 4% of tumor mass but concluded that targeting basal cells leads to complete tumor collapse, contradicting the expectation that such a minor population would have limited impact.

No long‑term outcome data after niche disruption

crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict synergistic targets downstream of CSF2 to mimic basal‑cell ablation without genetic manipulation.

crispro_opportunity.description: SEGER outperforms boundary‑based decontamination methods, offering a more accurate spatial transcriptomics pipeline.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We just say forget the boundary, assign transcripts to cells as link assignment, and that's our big difference."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate SEGER‑processed data to improve downstream computational modeling of spatial niches.

crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate population‑based kinetic curves to predict full dosimetry from limited time points, reducing patient burden.

talk_metadata.session_title: Targeted Alpha and Beta Emitting Radiopharmaceuticals: Multidisciplinary Precision Radiation Medicine
talk_metadata.talk_title: Clinical advances and opportunities in the treatment of prostate cancer using alpha and beta emitting radiopharmaceuticals
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "30% of patients did not respond or were resistant to treatment"
Speaker said: "PSMA RPT is not curative"
Speaker said: "Xerostomia after one cycle approached 100% with multiple cycles"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Salivary gland uptake leading to near‑universal xerostomia with repeated cycles
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: 70% of patients experienced xerostomia after one cycle; approaching 100% after multiple cycles
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted "very notable is that 70% of patients experienced xerostomia after one cycle"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: PSMA‑RPT in earlier disease lines (PSMA‑4, PSMA‑ADDITION)
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of overall survival benefit despite radiologic PFS improvement, likely due to crossover and suboptimal patient selection

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: PSMA‑targeted lutetium‑177 in chemotherapy‑naïve APMR
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolled patients after progression on one ARPI without requiring baseline PSMA intensity beyond minimal uptake
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Quantitative PSMA SUV cutoff or FDG discordance screening
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Overall survival unchanged likely due to high crossover rate

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: PSMA‑targeted lutetium‑177 added to ADT + ARPI in hormone‑sensitive disease
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Broad inclusion of hormone‑sensitive patients without stratifying by PSMA expression level
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Standardized PSMA intensity threshold
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: No OS benefit, possibly diluted by heterogeneous PSMA expression

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker highlighted unpredictable daughter isotopes and off‑target salivary gland toxicity, suggesting the chemistry may be circumvented by alternative linkers or shielding strategies
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Limited global production of Actinium‑225
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Use of same PSMA ligand (PSMA‑617) for both beta and alpha therapy may not address salivary gland uptake differences

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker presented robust overall survival benefit of lutetium‑PSMA in VISION, then later emphasized that "PSMA RPT is not curative" despite the same data, creating a tension between efficacy claims and curative expectations

key_data_gaps: Lack of OS benefit in earlier‑line trials (PSMA‑4, PSMA‑ADDITION)
Limited data on long‑term toxicity of repeated Actinium‑225 cycles

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate FDG‑PSMA discordance and TP53 mutation status into eligibility algorithms for PSMA‑RPT to reduce the 30% primary resistance rate.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted "30% of patients did not respond or were resistant" and mentioned low PSMA uptake, FDG discordance, and TP53 mutations as possible causes.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate imaging and genomic data to generate a composite resistance score for trial enrollment.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine Actinium‑225 PSMA with salivary‑gland protective agents (e.g., amifostine) or novel PSMA ligands that reduce off‑target uptake.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted xerostomia as a dose‑limiting toxicity for Actinium‑225.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Predictive modeling of ligand‑organ binding can prioritize chemistry modifications that minimize salivary gland dose.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design adaptive trials that re‑biopsy and re‑image after two cycles to adjust dosing based on dynamic PSMA expression changes.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned that response assessment is typically after two cycles using PSA and interim PSMA PET, but no formal adaptive design exists.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate adaptive dosing scenarios and estimate power gains.

crispro_opportunity.description: NEO811 shows activity in acquired resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors (G323E mutation) and intrinsic resistance (low HIF-2 alpha, high HIF-1 alpha).
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: NEO811 still retained full activity [in G323E mutation model]... NEO811 showed robust tumor growth inhibition, and again, a little bit better than just HIF-2 alpha alone. Again, these PDXs, they're serially transplanted over time. So we're doing the PD assessment on these, and they may now express a little HIF-2 compared to the publication, because again, belzutifan did show some activity. But we're very excited because NEO811 not only shows activity in acquired resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors, but also intrinsic resistance, showing this pan-HIF activity may really be a driver in the clinic.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO's platform can identify pan-HIF activity and its implications in resistance settings, potentially guiding combination strategies.
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: status of NEO811 trials in clear cell renal cell carcinoma

crispro_opportunity.description: NEO811 shows combination effect with belzutifan and potential for combinations with other standards of care.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So even though we are encouraged as the single agent studies, we wanted to then go in and say, can we do anything with combinations with other standard of cares, really to hit these pathways a little bit harder? And so giving the clinical success of HIF-2 alpha inhibitors, especially belzutifan recently, we wanted to test NEO811 and belzutifan in combination... not only adding a HIF-2 alpha inhibitor with ARNT, so they're targeting the exact same pathway, is that hopefully in the future, this will open us up to other combinations of other standards of care.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze synergistic effects of NEO811 with other standards of care, identifying optimal combination partners for CRCC.
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: ongoing trials for belzutifan in CRCC

talk_metadata.talk_title: Molecular glue degrader targeting the CCNE1

key_data_gaps: clinical development strategy and biomarkers beyond CCNE1 amplification

crispro_opportunity.description: While CCNE1 amplification is a strong biomarker, some amplified cell lines show reduced sensitivity, potentially due to P16 expression or other factors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Yes. So one interesting phenomenon is that cyclin E1 amplified cancers are almost always P16 high, so they express an endogenous inhibitor of CDK4/6. So it appears that, and there's also evidence for that, that there is a downside of having both hyperactive CDK2 and active CDK4/6 on top of it. So it's either/or in most cases. There was, I think in this panel, two exceptions, which had low P16, and this explains that then CDK4/6 is active at the same time as CDK2 and pure cyclin E1 degradation doesn't work.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the interplay of CCNE1 amplification, P16 expression, and CDK4/6 activity to refine patient stratification for MRT55811.
external_validation_needed: TCGA data analysis: correlation of CCNE1 amplification, P16 expression, and CDK4/6 activity in various cancer types
ClinicalTrials.gov: patient selection criteria for MRT55811 trials

crispro_opportunity.description: The CCNE1 degrader shows activity in models resistant to CDK2 inhibitors, suggesting a potential to overcome resistance to existing therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: CDK2 inhibitors, interestingly, are a bit different. So they do show preferential activity for cyclin E1 amplified cell lines, and that's more obvious in the lower plot where you can see the GI50s. But there are significant overlaps, so there's also a lot of activity in cell lines that are not amplified. We have seen this with multiple CDK2 inhibitors, not all are shown here.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify patient populations that have failed or are resistant to CDK2 inhibitors but may benefit from CCNE1 degradation.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: response rates of CDK2 inhibitors in CCNE1 amplified cancers
Biomarker analysis: identify patients with CCNE1 amplification who are refractory to CDK2 inhibitors

crispro_opportunity.description: HuR dependency is strongly correlated with BRAF mutations, suggesting BRAF mutant CRC as a key indication, but other BRAF mutant cancers may also be sensitive.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Analysis of that data showed a strong and positive correlation between HUR dependency and BRAF. Since BRAF mutations are prevalent in skin and colorectal cancers, we suppose that a BRAF mutant colorectal cancer might be particularly sensitive to HUR degradation.

crispro_opportunity.description: HuR degrader shows comparable efficacy to BRAF inhibitors in BRAF-mutant CRC, including dabrafenib-resistant models, suggesting it can overcome or bypass resistance.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: BRAF dabrafenib-resistant MDSDN cells were generated through long-time exposure. As expected, these resistant cells showed a clear loss of sensitivity to dabrafenib compared with this parental compound. In contrast, when treated with our HUR degrader Both parental compound and dabrafenib-resistant cells displayed strong and comparable reductions in cell viabilities.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify patients with BRAF-mutant CRC who have developed resistance to BRAF inhibitors and may benefit from HuR degradation therapy.

talk_metadata.talk_title: RAS PI3K alpha breaker

key_data_gaps: precise mechanism of how BB010203 interferes with RS2-HER2 complex formation
long-term efficacy and safety of BB010203 in combination therapies
activity of BB010203 in HER2 mutant models

crispro_opportunity.description: BB010203 shows strong combination potential with HER2-targeted agents (tucatinib, trastuzumab) in HER2-amplified models, potentially overcoming shortcomings of existing therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So to test that in BT474 cell lines, which is a HER2-amplified model, we gave the combined approach of 10203 in combination with tucatinib here on the left or trastuzumab here on the right. And what you can see in blue is strong monotherapy effects with 10203. But when you combine that with the other agent, it led to strong regressions... And when given in combination with the HER2 targeting antibody trastuzumab, led to strong regressions, and again, outperformed the combination of tucatinib plus trastuzumab.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the synergistic effects of BB010203 with HER2-targeted agents and other therapies in HER2-amplified cancers to optimize combination strategies.
external_validation_needed: ClinicalTrials.gov: status of Breaker-101 clinical trial
Company pipeline databases: development status of BB010203 and its combinations

crispro_opportunity.description: The disruption of non-canonical RAS signaling by BB010203 may address resistance mechanisms to canonical RAS inhibitors and HER2-targeted therapies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So this data shows by genetic mechanisms that the PIK3CA RAS interaction is essential for phospho-AKT activation and survival. So to understand what RAS isoforms were driving this dependency, the first thing we did is we screened our panel of our two amplified lines against with 10203 compared to the pan-RAS inhibitor 6236. And what we observed is that with 10203, as expected, we saw complete phospho-AKT suppression. But with the pan-RAS inhibitor RMC-6236, we saw moderate suppression of phospho-AKT, despite seeing complete phospho-ERK suppression. This was seen across a larger panel of lines, here shown on the right. So this data to us indicated that the phospho-AKT activity in these lines was not likely driven from canonical RAS proteins, which were inhibited by the RMC-6236, but likely coming from non-canonical proteins, which were not hit by 6236.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify resistance mechanisms to HER2-targeted therapies and canonical RAS inhibitors that are driven by non-canonical RAS signaling, and assess BB010203's potential to overcome them.
external_validation_needed: PubMed: research on resistance mechanisms to HER2-targeted therapies and canonical RAS inhibitors
Clinical trial data: identify patients with HER2-amplified cancers who have progressed on standard therapies

talk_metadata.talk_title: Catalytic inhibitor of the KAT2A/B protein

crispro_opportunity.description: KAT2 inhibition upregulates interferon responses, suggesting potential for combination with immune-oncology therapies like BCMA CD3 bispecific antibodies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So we then utilized a mouse syngeneic model of multiple myeloma to test tumor growth inhibition in combination with BCMA CD3 bispecific antibody. ... But additionally, what you can see also is that we do extend the tumor growth delay in a dose-dependent manner when we add our KAT2 inhibitor with the bispecific antibody. And this is reflected in the survival curve shown on the right. So we're very excited about the idea that this could potentially open up opportunities to combine our KAT2 inhibitor with IO therapies to deepen responses in multiple myeloma.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the immune-modulatory effects of KAT2 inhibitors and identify synergistic combinations with T-cell engagers and other immunotherapies.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: immune cell infiltration and response in patients treated with KAT2 inhibitors
Preclinical studies: evaluate combination of KAT2 inhibitor with checkpoint inhibitors or CAR-T therapies in relevant models

crispro_opportunity.description: KAT2 inhibition shows synergistic activity with IMiDs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide) in multiple myeloma, suggesting it can enhance the efficacy of standard of care treatments.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So we combined our KAT2 inhibitor with either lenalidomide or pomalidomide in a combination matrix experiment for proliferation in vitro. ... So when you get this output data, if you get a value between zero to one, this is considered additive, and you can see this by the column on the right where we have the self-cross of our compound with itself and those values range there. If you have a value that's greater than one, this suggests you can have synergy between the two compounds, and you can see for both our KAT2 inhibitor combination with lenalidomide and pomalidomide, most of the cell lines we observe synergistic scores.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify patient populations with multiple myeloma who may benefit from combination therapy with KAT2 inhibitors and IMiDs.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: response rates of IMiDs in multiple myeloma patients
Biomarker analysis: identify patients with multiple myeloma who are refractory or have limited response to IMiDs

crispro_opportunity.description: The SAGA complex and KAT2A/B are identified as top dependencies for multiple myeloma, suggesting a potential for biomarker-driven patient selection.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So what you're seeing here is this is a bit of the DepMap data where we were asking in multiple myeloma cells versus every other tumor type, what are the protein complexes that myeloma cells are most dependent on? And so we used the CRISPR dropout scores here. We used a quorum protein complex analysis, and what you can see here is that the SAGA complex and other KAT2-containing complexes showed up as the top dependencies for multiple myeloma, as are highlighted here in the red.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the dependency of various cancer types on SAGA and KAT2A/B to identify optimal indications and biomarkers for KAT2 inhibitors.
external_validation_needed: DepMap data analysis: comprehensive assessment of KAT2A/B dependency across all cancer types

talk_metadata.talk_title: Discovery of a new class of mutant-targeted catalytic RAS oncogene inhibitor

key_data_gaps: stoichiometry and catalytic efficacy of RMO-55 in cellular contexts

crispro_opportunity.description: RMO-55 demonstrates activity in models of acquired resistance to RAS on inhibitors, particularly those involving KRAS amplification or increased expression.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So going back to the clinical resistance I started with, one of the major mechanisms of clinical resistance, at least in terms of where we can detect a mechanism of resistance from genomic alterations in ctDNA, is KRAS amplification of the mutant associated with the G12 mutant allele... However, RMO-55 at 10 MPK was able to produce deep and very durable regressions in these resistant models. In a related model, we looked at the ability of RMO-55 to restore tumor control in tumors that were already outgrowing on deraxone rasib. And you can see here, these tumors initially showed regression, but then started to grow out with continued deraxone rasib treatment. However, in the group that we switched over to RMO-55, we were able to restore tumor control and had durable regressions out to the end of the study.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify resistance mechanisms to RAS inhibitors, including those involving RAS amplification or overexpression, and assess RMO-55's potential to overcome them.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: identify patients with RAS-mutant cancers who have progressed on RAS inhibitors
PubMed: research on resistance mechanisms to RAS inhibitors, including amplification and overexpression

crispro_opportunity.description: RMO-55's catalytic mechanism and potential for broader RAS inhibition suggest opportunities for combination with other RAS inhibitors or therapies targeting downstream effectors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: So the G13 and some of the Q61, we have steric occlusion activity analogous to the steric occlusion activity that I described at wild type. Mm-hmm. Okay. Thank you. A very nice talk. So you show that the RM-055 has stimulate the GTP hydrolysis compared to daxonesib. So compared to direct combo with the RAS-off inhibitor, does this RM-055 provide the advantage for the combo with the RAS-off inhibitor? We are currently looking at a number of different combinations, and that is obviously one we're interested in.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the synergistic effects of RMO-55 with RAS-off inhibitors and other therapies targeting the RAS pathway to optimize combination strategies.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: response rates of RAS-off inhibitors in RAS-mutant cancers
Preclinical studies: evaluate combination of RMO-55 with RAS-off inhibitors in relevant models

crispro_opportunity.description: RMO-55 represents a new class of RAS inhibitor with a catalytic mechanism, offering a distinct approach compared to existing RAS on and RAS off inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Thus far, there's really been two major classes of RAS inhibitor. The first class is the RAS off inhibitors. These are switch two pocket binders... The other class of inhibitors is the molecular glues or tricomplex inhibitors. These bind exclusively to the on state of RAS... Today, I would like to introduce a third class of inhibitors. Oh, my goodness. That basically catalytically inactivate RAS and then essentially stimulate the hydrolysis of RAS GTP to RAS GDP and mutant RAS, and in so doing, mimic the activity of natural gaps at wild-type RAS.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can highlight the unique catalytic mechanism of RMO-55 and its potential advantages over existing RAS inhibitor classes in addressing resistance and unmet needs.
external_validation_needed: Market analysis: competitive landscape of RAS inhibitors
Clinical trial data: comparative efficacy and safety of RMO-55 versus existing RAS inhibitors

talk_metadata.talk_title: RNA‑targeted small molecules inhibiting c‑MYC translation
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We do not affect levels of the Myc mRNA transcript in these cells, which is also consistent with a translational mechanism."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lymphopenia observed in mice suggests off‑target impact on immune cells not captured by in‑vitro selectivity assays.
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted lymphopenia as a major challenge, implying immune‑cell toxicity.

competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single nutrient‑dependent activation mechanism may limit efficacy across tumor microenvironments.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed rapid MYC protein degradation within 30 min, then concluded that the compound is broadly tolerable with no body‑weight loss, despite early immune toxicity signals.

No human PK/PD data

talk_metadata.talk_title: Inducer‑amplifier strategy for TP53‑mutant cancers
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The interaction was synergistic specifically in TP53 mutant and knockout cells"
Speaker said: "Adding a WEE1 inhibitor after the priming increased cell death from 30% to nearly 80%"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: PARP inhibitor monotherapy in TP53‑mutant tumors
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: TP53 loss leads to reliance on base excision repair; PARP inhibition alone does not induce lethal DNA damage without additional replication stress
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that PARP inhibitor alone showed limited efficacy despite high DNA damage markers

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: First‑in‑human trial of the inducer‑amplifier regimen (no NCT provided)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Base excision repair inhibition (TAS‑102) + PARP inhibition + WEE1 inhibition
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No stratification by TP53 mutation status reported in early trial design
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: TP53 mutation confirmation and BER activity assay
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Early safety data only; efficacy endpoints not yet powered

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Inducer‑amplifier three‑drug regimen (TAS‑102 + PARP inhibitor + WEE1 inhibitor)
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Relies on sequential dosing schedule; any deviation may reduce synergy, indicating a procedural vulnerability
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Complex three‑drug schedule may limit patient adherence and increase drug‑drug interaction risk

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that PARP inhibition alone increased DNA damage but did not cause cell death, yet concluded that adding WEE1 inhibitor converts damage to lethal mitotic catastrophe

key_data_gaps: No randomized clinical data yet
Unclear toxicity profile of three‑drug combination in humans

crispro_opportunity.description: Reposition existing PARP inhibitors for TP53‑mutant cancers using the inducer‑amplifier framework
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker demonstrated preclinical synergy of PARP inhibition with TAS‑102 and WEE1 blockade
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synthetic lethal networks to identify optimal dosing sequences for existing PARP inhibitors

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate TP53 mutation testing and BER activity biomarkers as eligibility criteria
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that mutant cells retain EDU longer, indicating a measurable biomarker
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can define quantitative thresholds for BER activity to enrich for responders

cited_competitors.name: Mosaic Therapeutics MDM2‑PARP inhibitor combo
cited_competitors.context: questioned during Q&A about wild‑type TP53 activation

unresolved_questions: What is the maximum tolerated dose of the three‑drug regimen in TP53‑mutant patients?

assets_to_track: PARP inhibitors under investigation for TP53‑mutant cancers

talk_metadata.talk_title: KBD‑1111: a selective, long‑acting, brain‑penetrant PARP‑1 inhibitor
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "KBD‑1111 shows 9,000‑fold selectivity for PARP‑1 over PARP‑2"
Speaker said: "In rats, QW dosing showed no significant reticulocyte decrease, unlike QD dosing"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: PARP‑2 inhibition drives hematologic toxicity (anemia, thrombocytopenia)
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted that olaparib caused significant reticulocyte reduction while KBD‑1111 QW regimen did not

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Phase 1 first‑in‑human trial of KBD‑1111 (no NCT provided)

trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: PARP‑1 activity or HRD status to enrich for responders

key_data_gaps: No human efficacy or CNS activity data yet

talk_metadata.talk_title: RAS and RAF targeting in MSS metastatic colorectal cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't have that data formally yet"

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Monell Chemical Senses Center / University of Pennsylvania
talk_metadata.talk_title: Chemosensory dysfunction in cancer: prevalence, impact and unmet needs
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Currently, we don't have pharmacological solutions to reverse the smell dysfunction or taste dysfunction that really works."
Speaker said: "We don't have good data in the EMR either, because in time‑pressured environments it is often deprioritized."
Speaker said: "There is no wonder drug here... the best we can do right now is nutrition counseling and self‑management strategies."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: lack of mechanistic understanding of how cancer therapies cause persistent taste and smell loss

key_data_gaps: No pharmacologic cure demonstrated
Limited longitudinal data on recovery of chemosensory function after therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Absence of validated biomarkers to identify patients at highest risk for chemosensory dysfunction, limiting targeted preventive or therapeutic interventions.

crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omic and clinical data to discover predictive signatures of chemosensory toxicity for specific regimens.

talk_metadata.talk_title: Taste bud homeostasis, cancer therapeutics and organoid models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't fully understand why a steady rundown of taste buds causes dysgeusia so quickly in patients."
Speaker said: "We still lack in vitro models for human taste epithelium."
Speaker said: "The smoothened antagonist knocks out taste buds, yet it is highly effective against cancer – a paradoxical effect."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: off‑target impact on taste progenitor proliferation leading to excess type‑I cells and loss of sweet perception
inhibition of progenitor differentiation into taste cells without affecting proliferation
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted loss of taste buds is a major cause of non‑compliance with these drugs.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: off‑target inhibition of KIT in taste progenitors shifts fate from sweet to bitter cells

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: KIT inhibition off‑target in taste epithelium
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker revealed that TKIs designed for VEGF/PDGF pathways unintentionally inhibit KIT in taste buds, causing sweet‑cell loss.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad use of anti‑angiogenic TKIs without biomarker‑selected dosing leads to pervasive dysgeusia.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that smoothened antagonists efficiently shrink tumors, then concluded that these drugs cause severe loss of taste buds – a contradiction between tumor efficacy and taste toxicity.

key_data_gaps: No human taste organoid data yet
Lack of clinical correlation between mouse taste phenotypes and patient dysgeusia

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify and model off‑target KIT inhibition in taste progenitors to design selective TKIs that spare taste function.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described how cabozantinib reduces sweet cells via KIT inhibition.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict off‑target kinase interactions and suggest scaffold modifications to retain anti‑angiogenic activity while preserving KIT signaling in taste.

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate chemosensory endpoints (e.g., taste‑bud imaging, patient‑reported dysgeusia) into early‑phase trials of Wnt or hedgehog inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that dysgeusia is a major cause of non‑compliance for these agents.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate patient‑level toxicity profiles to optimize dosing schedules that minimize taste loss.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage human taste organoid platform to screen candidate drugs for sensory toxicity before clinical testing.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized the lack of human taste organoid models and the need for such platforms.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate organoid assay readouts with computational predictions to prioritize low‑toxicity compounds.

vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: weight loss and peripheral neuropathy observed in long‑term serine/glycine‑depleted mice

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No stratification of tumors by PHGDH expression or serine synthesis pathway status
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: PHGDH amplification / de novo serine synthesis activity

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Dietary serine/glycine restriction as a therapeutic modality
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Efficacy is limited to tumors lacking de novo serine synthesis; tumors can bypass by up‑regulating PHGDH
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad, non‑biomarker‑selected dietary intervention dilutes signal in heterogeneous patient populations

key_data_gaps: No human randomized trial data
Lack of biomarker‑stratified efficacy data
Long‑term toxicity (e.g., neuropathy) not fully characterized

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patients with low PHGDH expression or loss of serine synthesis enzymes to enrich for responders to serine/glycine restriction.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "if you look in cBioPortal, many tumors show amplification of the serine synthesis pathway" and "in tumors with PHGDH up‑regulation the diet has no impact"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate genomic, transcriptomic and metabolomic data to predict serine synthesis dependency and select patients for dietary trials.

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine PHGDH inhibitors with serine/glycine‑restricted diet to overcome primary resistance in PHGDH‑high tumors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "the answer there clearly would be, let's use an inhibitor of this serine synthesis pathway"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model synergistic effects of metabolic enzyme inhibition plus nutrient restriction to prioritize combination regimens.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design a biomarker‑driven phase 2 trial that enrolls only PHGDH‑low or serine‑auxotrophic tumors and monitors circulating serine/glycine levels.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we have done some trials in normal healthy volunteers and found that you can also reduce circulating levels of serine and glycine"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO’s predictive modeling to define inclusion criteria and power calculations based on expected metabolic response.

cited_competitors.name: Vander Heiden Lab
cited_competitors.context: cited for foundational studies on serine metabolism
